title: A Loverן¿½s Quarrel
content:
I.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, what a dawn of day!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How the March sun feels like May!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All is blue again
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ After last nightן¿½s rain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the South dries the hawthorn-spray.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only, my Loveן¿½s away!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½d as lief that the blue were grey,

II.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Runnels, which rillets swell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must be dancing down the dell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a foaming head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the beryl bed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Paven smooth as a hermitן¿½s cell;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each with a tale to tell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Could my Love but attend as well.

III.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dearest, three months ago!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When we lived blocked-up with snow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the wind would edge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In and in his wedge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In, as far as the point could go,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not to our ingle, though,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where we loved each the other so!

IV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Laughs with so little cause!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We devised games out of straws.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We would try and trace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One anotherן¿½s face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the ash, as an artist draws;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Free on each otherן¿½s flaws,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How we chattered like two church daws!

V.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whatן¿½s in the ן¿½Timesן¿½? a scold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the Emperor deep and cold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He has taken a bride
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To his gruesome side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s as fair as himself is bold:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There they sit ermine-stoled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she powders her hair with gold.

VI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fancy the Pampasן¿½ sheen!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Miles and miles of gold and green
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the sunflowers blow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a solid glow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, to break now and then the screen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Black neck and eyeballs keen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Up a wild horse leaps between!

VII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Try, will our table turn?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lay your hands there light, and yearn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till the yearning slips
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Throן¿½ the finger-tips
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a fire which a few discern,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a very few feel burn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the rest, they may live and learn!

VIII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then we would up and pace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a change, about the place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each with arm oן¿½er neck:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis our quarter-deck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are seamen in woeful case.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Help in the ocean-space!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or, if no help, weן¿½ll embrace.

IX.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ See, how she looks now, dressed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a sledging-cap and vest!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis a huge fur cloak
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like a reindeerן¿½s yoke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Falls the lappet along the breast:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sleeves for her arms to rest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or to hang, as my Love likes best.

X.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Teach me to flirt a fan
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the Spanish ladies can,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or I tint your lip
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a burnt stickן¿½s tip
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you turn into such a man!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just the two spots that span
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Half the bill of the young male swan.

XI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dearest, three months ago
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the mesmerizer Snow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his handן¿½s first sweep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Put the earth to sleep:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas a time when the heart could show
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All, how was earth to know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Neath the mute handן¿½s to-and-fro?

XII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dearest, three months ago
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When we loved each other so,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lived and loved the same
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till an evening came
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When a shaft from the devilן¿½s bow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pierced to our ingle-glow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the friends were friend and foe!

XIII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not from the heart beneath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas a bubble born of breath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Neither sneer nor vaunt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor reproach nor taunt.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ See a word, how it severeth!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, power of life and death
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the tongue, as the Preacher saith!

XIV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Woman, and will you cast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a word, quite off at last
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Me, your own, your You,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since, as truth is true,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was You all the happy past,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Me do you leave aghast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the memories We amassed?

XV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Love, if you knew the light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That your soul casts in my sight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How I look to you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the pure and true
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the beauteous and the right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bear with a momentן¿½s spite
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When a mere mote threats the white!

XVI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What of a hasty word?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is the fleshly heart not stirred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By a wormן¿½s pin-prick
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where its roots are quick?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ See the eye, by a flyן¿½s foot blurred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ear, when a straw is heard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scratch the brainן¿½s coat of curd!

XVII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Foul be the world or fair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More or less, how can I care?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis the world the same
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For my praise or blame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And endurance is easy there.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wrong in the one thing rare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, it is hard to bear!

XVIII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hereן¿½s the spring back or close,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the almond-blossom blows:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We shall have the word
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a minor third
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is none but the cuckoo knows:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heaps of the guelder-rose!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I must bear with it, I suppose.

XIX.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Could but November come,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were the noisy birds struck dumb
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the warning slash
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of his driverן¿½s-lash
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I would laugh like the valiant Thumb
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Facing the castle glum
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the giantן¿½s fee-faw-fum!

XX.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then, were the world well stripped
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the gear wherein equipped
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We can stand apart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heart dispense with heart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the sun, with the flowers unnipped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, the worldן¿½s hangings ripped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We were both in a bare-walled crypt!

XXI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each in the crypt would cry
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But one freezes here! and why?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When a heart, as chill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½At my own would thrill
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Back to life, and its fires out-fly?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Heart, shall we live or die?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The rest, . . . settle by-and-by!ן¿½

XXII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, sheן¿½d efface the score,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And forgive me as before.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is twelve oן¿½clock:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I shall hear her knock
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the worst of a stormן¿½s uproar
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I shall pull her through the door
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I shall have her for evermore!
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Robert Browning delivers a powerful performance in "A Loverן¿½s Quarrel"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
